notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your Chase Sapphire Preferred (ending 8600) shows a $1,840 balance in your transaction data, and the good news is that everything I can see suggests this is simply your normal monthly spending float — not revolving debt.
Looking at the transactions on that card from last month, the charges align perfectly with your everyday lifestyle: groceries at Costco and Trader Joe's, dining at Tavolata, Ramen Danbo, and Oddfellows Cafe, your ClassPass membership, Adobe, Netflix, Lyft, AT&T Wireless, Xfinity, and your SFO work trip (Alaska Airlines + Avis). These are all routine, expected expenses — the kind of spending that earns you Sapphire Preferred points while costing you nothing in interest if paid in full.
What to verify first
Before deciding on a payoff strategy, the single most important thing to confirm is whether a statement payment has already been made or is scheduled. Since your account balances are currently showing $0 across all connected accounts (a data gap I want to flag — this likely reflects a syncing lag rather than actual zero balances), I can't confirm your current checking balance or payment status directly.
Note that account balances from aggregators (such as Plaid) may be delayed or incomplete. Before making a transfer or payment, please verify the exact available balance directly in your bank account. If the balance is different, share the updated amount here and I can refine the recommendation.
My recommendation
Given what the data shows, autopay for the full statement balance is the ideal setup here. Here's why this fits your situation well:
You earn rewards without paying interest — the Sapphire Preferred gives you strong travel and dining points, and paying in full monthly means you capture all of that at zero cost
It builds payment history — on-time, full payments are the single largest factor in your credit score (~35% of FICO), and autopay makes this automatic
The $1,840 is well within your income capacity — your Microsoft payroll deposits $1,840 semi-monthly net, and you received a $1,200 freelance payment from Figma last month, so servicing this balance is clearly manageable
You don't need to rush a manual payment today unless the statement due date is approaching and autopay isn't already set. The balance isn't debt — it's just your current billing cycle's charges waiting to be cleared.
Would you like help estimating the points you're earning on this spending, or would it be more useful to look at whether your current income and cash flow comfortably cover your recurring monthly obligations?

Results aren't guaranteed. See disclosures.

Jan
